module: Module 15: Treatment of Psychological Disorders — Advanced Applications

sections.text: Welcome back to AP Psychology. Module Fifteen — treatment of psychological disorders, advanced applications. Last module we surveyed the toolbox. Today — we choose the right tool for the right problem, for the right person.

Last module — the toolbox. Five therapies, biomedical treatments, the whole map. Today — choosing. Because here's the thing. Handing someone with PTSD a Freudian couch and a fifty-year-old theory just doesn't work. Handing someone with schizophrenia a thought record won't fix the problem. Treatment isn't one-size-fits-all. It's a match. Match the disorder. Match the person. Match the culture, the age, the family. That's what good clinicians actually do. So today — three case studies, what the evidence says, group and family therapy, cultural considerations, and special notes for kids and teens.

Three things today. One — for each major disorder, what does the evidence say is the front-line treatment. Two — three case studies. We'll look at a presentation, and you'll see how a clinician thinks through the recommendation. Three — group therapy, family therapy, cultural considerations, and special notes for children and adolescents. By the end you'll be able to read a vignette and explain why one treatment fits better than another.

First — what does the evidence base actually say. Anxiety disorders and specific phobias — behavioral therapy, especially exposure and systematic desensitization, is usually first-line. Depression — CBT for mild to moderate cases. CBT plus an SSRI for moderate to severe. OCD — CBT with a specific variant called exposure and response prevention, ERP. The person is exposed to the trigger and prevented from doing the compulsion. SSRIs as adjunct. PTSD — trauma-focused CBT, EMDR (eye movement desensitization and reprocessing), and prolonged exposure. Schizophrenia — antipsychotic medication is the primary treatment. Therapy and family education are supportive, not standalone. Bipolar disorder — mood stabilizer like lithium is the foundation. Psychotherapy is for psychosocial support. Borderline personality disorder — DBT, dialectical behavior therapy, has the strongest evidence. The pattern — for anxiety-spectrum problems, behavioral and cognitive tools lead. For psychotic and severe mood disorders, medication leads, with therapy supporting.

Case one. Maya, hypothetical seventeen-year-old. She has had an escalating fear of dogs since being bitten by a neighbor's dog when she was nine. School performance is fine. Friends are fine. But she now refuses to walk routes where dogs might be off-leash, and a recent encounter caused a panic episode. Specific phobia. What does the evidence say. Systematic desensitization or graduated exposure therapy. Build a fear hierarchy from least to most threatening — looking at a photo of a dog, watching a video, being in the same room as a small calm dog on a leash, eventually petting one. Pair each step with relaxation. Climb the ladder. Most specific phobias respond well, often in eight to twelve sessions. Medication is generally not needed. Why this works — the original fear was learned through classical conditioning. Treatment uses the same learning system to teach the brain a new association. Dog equals safe. Outcome is usually excellent.

Case two. Jordan, hypothetical sixteen-year-old. Won't get out of bed. Lost interest in soccer, which he used to love. Sleeping twelve hours a day. Six weeks of low mood, low energy, and feeling worthless. Grades have started to slip. This presentation looks like a major depressive episode. What does the evidence say. For mild to moderate depression in an adolescent, evidence-based therapy first — typically CBT or interpersonal therapy. Behavioral activation is huge — gently rebuilding a daily structure with meaningful activities. Thought records to challenge the negative self-talk. If symptoms are moderate to severe, or if therapy alone isn't working after six to eight weeks, an SSRI may be added — fluoxetine has the most evidence in adolescents. We also screen carefully for safety. And family is involved — depression in a teenager is everyone's problem to support, not just the teenager's. End point — most adolescents with depression recover fully when treated. The path back exists.

Case three. Alex, hypothetical eighteen-year-old college freshman. Intrusive thoughts of contamination — convinced surfaces in the dorm are dangerous. Washes hands forty times a day. Hands are cracked and bleeding. Spends two hours a night cleaning the room before being able to sleep. This is OCD. What does the evidence say. The treatment of choice is exposure and response prevention, ERP, a specialized form of CBT. The person is gradually exposed to the trigger — touching a doorknob, for example — and then prevented from doing the compulsion — no immediate hand-washing. The anxiety spikes, then naturally falls. The brain learns the feared catastrophe doesn't happen. SSRIs, often at higher doses than for depression, can be added as adjunct. ERP is hard work. It feels counterintuitive at first. But the response rates are excellent, and Alex's quality of life can change dramatically within a few months.

Your turn. A new case. A college sophomore reports flashbacks, nightmares, and severe distress whenever she hears fireworks, ever since a violent incident last summer. She avoids the area where it happened. She is hypervigilant and irritable. Question one — what disorder is this likely describing. Question two — what is the front-line evidence-based treatment. Pause. Decide. Press play.

Welcome back. The disorder — post-traumatic stress disorder, PTSD. Flashbacks, avoidance, hyperarousal, after a clearly traumatic event. The front-line treatments — trauma-focused CBT, EMDR (eye movement desensitization and reprocessing), or prolonged exposure therapy. All three help the brain reprocess the traumatic memory so it stops triggering as if the danger were still present. Medication, typically an SSRI, can be added as support. Notice the pattern across all four cases. Identify the disorder. Match it to the evidence-based treatment. Adjust for the person.

Now beyond individual therapy. Group therapy. Several people with similar issues meet together with one or two trained therapists. Why it works. Shared experience — the relief of realizing you are not the only one. Normalization — your problem is human, not unique. Peer feedback — sometimes hearing it from someone in the same boat lands harder than from a professional. Lower cost. Group therapy works well for substance abuse, social anxiety, grief and bereavement, and eating disorders. It is not a substitute for individual therapy in severe cases — it is a complement, or a step-down.

Family therapy and couples therapy. The systemic insight — sometimes the person showing the symptom is expressing a problem in the whole family system. A teenager's behavior may be the visible part of a tension between the parents. Structural family therapy looks at boundaries, hierarchies, and roles. Who is in charge. Who is enmeshed with whom. Who is excluded. Couples therapy similarly treats the relationship as the patient — communication patterns, attachment styles, conflict cycles. The point — individual problems often have relational roots. Treating only the individual when the system is the cause is like bailing out a boat without fixing the leak.

Two final pieces. Cultural considerations — therapists must develop cultural competence. Trust gaps are real — some communities have very good historical reasons to be wary of mental health institutions. Language access matters. Stigma around mental health varies enormously by culture. A skilled therapist adapts — pace, communication style, family involvement, language, even the framing of the problem. One-size-fits-all therapy is bad therapy. Children and adolescents — special considerations. Younger children often respond best to play therapy or art therapy, which gives them a non-verbal way to express what they cannot put into words. Family involvement is essential — kids do not exist in isolation. And on medication — SSRIs for adolescents carry an FDA black-box warning about potential increased suicidality early in treatment. They are still used, and often help, but with very close monitoring in the first weeks. Therapy first, medication carefully second, is the typical adolescent approach.

Recap. Six things. One — anxiety and phobias respond best to behavioral therapy — exposure and systematic desensitization. Two — depression responds to CBT, plus an SSRI for moderate to severe cases. Three — OCD needs exposure and response prevention, ERP, often with an SSRI. PTSD needs trauma-focused CBT, EMDR, or prolonged exposure. Four — schizophrenia is medication-primary, antipsychotics, with therapy as supportive. Bipolar is mood-stabilizer-primary. Borderline responds to DBT. Five — group therapy normalizes and reduces cost; family and couples therapy treat the system, not just the person. Six — culture matters, and children and adolescents need play, family involvement, and careful medication monitoring. The takeaway — match treatment to disorder, to person, to context. That's the whole game.

Important — this video is the overview. To actually master Module Fifteen, here's the path. One — read Myers Psychology for AP, the sections on evidence-based treatment matching, group and family therapy, and cultural considerations. Two — go to AP Classroom and try fifteen multiple-choice questions where you read a case description and pick the best treatment. AP loves this format. Three — do the assignment in your dashboard — six clinical vignettes, match each to the correct front-line treatment with a one-sentence rationale. Four — book a check-in with your advisor if any of the disorder-treatment pairings blur. Coming up — Module Sixteen — synthesis and AP exam strategy. We tie the whole psychology unit together and walk through how to actually crush the free-response questions on the exam.
